(**Technology, Entertainment, Design**[[7]](#cite_note-about_TED-7)) is an American-Canadian [nonprofit](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization")[[7]](#cite_note-about_TED-7) media organization that posts international talks online for free distribution under the slogan "Ideas Change Everything" (previously "Ideas Worth Spreading").[[8]](#cite_note-Introducing_TED's_new_tagline-8) It was founded by [Richard Saul Wurman](/wiki/Richard_Saul_Wurman "Richard Saul Wurman") and [Harry Marks](/wiki/Harry_Marks_(broadcast_designer) "Harry Marks (broadcast designer)") in February 1984[[2]](#cite_note-ted_history-2) as a technology conference, in which Mickey Schulhof gave a demo of the [compact disc](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc") that was invented in October 1982.[[5]](#cite_note-first_conf-5) Its main conference has been held annually since 1990.[[6]](#cite_note-first_annual-6)[[9]](#cite_note-:1-9) It covers almost all topics—from science to literature to business to [global issues](/wiki/List_of_global_issues "List of global issues")—in more than 100 languages.[[7]](#cite_note-about_TED-7)
since July 2019 (originally by the non-profit [Sapling Foundation](/wiki/Sapling_Foundation "Sapling Foundation")).[[1]](#cite_note-howtedworks-1)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

**TED Conferences, LLC** (**Technology, Entertainment, Design**[[7]](#cite_note-about_TED-7)) is an American-Canadian [nonprofit](/wiki/Nonprofit_organization "Nonprofit organization")[[7]](#cite_note-about_TED-7) media organization that posts international talks online for free distribution under the slogan "Ideas Change Everything" (previously "Ideas Worth Spreading").[[8]](#cite_note-Introducing_TED's_new_tagline-8) It was founded by [Richard Saul Wurman](/wiki/Richard_Saul_Wurman "Richard Saul Wurman") and [Harry Marks](/wiki/Harry_Marks_(broadcast_designer) "Harry Marks (broadcast designer)") in February 1984[[2]](#cite_note-ted_history-2) as a technology conference, in which Mickey Schulhof gave a demo of the [compact disc](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc") that was invented in October 1982.[[5]](#cite_note-first_conf-5) Its main conference has been held annually since 1990.[[6]](#cite_note-first_annual-6)[[9]](#cite_note-:1-9) It covers almost all topics—from science to literature to business to [global issues](/wiki/List_of_global_issues "List of global issues")—in more than 100 languages.[[7]](#cite_note-about_TED-7)

TED's early emphasis was on technology and design, consistent with its [Silicon Valley](/wiki/Silicon_Valley "Silicon Valley") origins. It later broadened to include scientific, cultural, political, humanitarian, and academic topics.[[10]](#cite_note-10) It has been curated by [Chris Anderson](/wiki/Chris_Anderson_(entrepreneur) "Chris Anderson (entrepreneur)"), a British-American businessman, through the non-profit **TED Foundation** since July 2019 (originally by the non-profit [Sapling Foundation](/wiki/Sapling_Foundation "Sapling Foundation")).[[1]](#cite_note-howtedworks-1)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The main TED conference has been held annually in [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"), British Columbia, Canada, at the [Vancouver Convention Centre](/wiki/Vancouver_Convention_Centre "Vancouver Convention Centre") since 2014. The first conferences from 1984 (TED1) through 2008 (TED2008) were held at the Monterey Conference Center in [Monterey, California](/wiki/Monterey,_California "Monterey, California").[[13]](#cite_note-13) From 2009 to 2014, it was held in [Long Beach, California](/wiki/Long_Beach,_California "Long Beach, California"), United States.[[14]](#cite_note-14) TED events are also held throughout North America and in Europe, Asia, and Africa, offering [live streaming](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") of the talks. TED returned to Monterey in 2021 with TEDMonterey. The talks address a wide range of topics within the research and practice of science and culture, often through storytelling.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

TED conferences are invitation-only events with an admission of $6,000.[[16]](#cite_note-:0-16)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=TED_(conference)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 1984–1999: Founding and early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=TED_(conference)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1984–1999: Founding and early years")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Bill_Clinton_talking_at_TED_2007.jpg/250px-Bill_Clinton_talking_at_TED_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bill_Clinton_talking_at_TED_2007.jpg)

[Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") addresses TED, 2007.

TED was conceived in 1984 by [Richard Saul Wurman](/wiki/Richard_Saul_Wurman "Richard Saul Wurman"), FAIA '76,[[18]](#cite_note-18) and co-founded by Emmy-winning broadcast and graphic designer [Harry Marks](/wiki/Harry_Marks_(broadcast_designer) "Harry Marks (broadcast designer)") and CBS President Emeritus [Frank Stanton](/wiki/Frank_Stanton_(executive) "Frank Stanton (executive)"). The conference featured demos of the [compact disc](/wiki/Compact_disc "Compact disc"), co-developed by [Philips](/wiki/Philips "Philips") and [Sony](/wiki/Sony "Sony"), and one of the first demonstrations of the [Apple Macintosh](/wiki/Apple_Macintosh "Apple Macintosh") computer.[[3]](#cite_note-addict-3)[[19]](#cite_note-forum-19) Presentations were given by the mathematician [Benoit Mandelbrot](/wiki/Benoit_Mandelbrot "Benoit Mandelbrot") and others such as [Nicholas Negroponte](/wiki/Nicholas_Negroponte "Nicholas Negroponte") and [Stewart Brand](/wiki/Stewart_Brand "Stewart Brand"). The event was financially unsuccessful; six years elapsed before a second conference was organized.[[20]](#cite_note-ted.com-20)

TED2 was held at the same Monterey Conference Center in California in 1990. From 1990 onward, a growing community of "TEDsters" gathered annually with Wurman leading the conference in Monterey until 2009,[[21]](#cite_note-21) when it was moved to [Long Beach, California](/wiki/Long_Beach,_California "Long Beach, California") due to a substantial increase in the number of those attending.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-LATimes-23) Speakers were initially drawn from the fields of expertise behind the [acronym](/wiki/Acronym "Acronym") TED; but during the 1990s, presenters broadened to include scientists, philosophers, musicians, religious leaders, philanthropists, and many others.[[20]](#cite_note-ted.com-20)

### 2000–present: Recent growth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=TED_(conference)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2000–present: Recent growth")]

In 2000, Wurman, looking for a successor at age 65, met with new-media entrepreneur and TED enthusiast [Chris Anderson](/wiki/Chris_Anderson_(entrepreneur) "Chris Anderson (entrepreneur)") to discuss future happenings. Anderson's UK media company [Future](/wiki/Future_plc "Future plc") bought TED for $14 million ($12 million in cash and $2 million in stocks). In November 2001, Anderson's non-profit The [Sapling Foundation](/wiki/Sapling_Foundation "Sapling Foundation") (motto: "fostering the spread of great ideas")[[1]](#cite_note-howtedworks-1) acquired TED from Future for £6m.[[24]](#cite_note-24) In February 2002, Anderson gave a TED Talk in which he explained his vision of the conference and his future role of [curator](/wiki/Curator "Curator").[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In 2012, TED community director Tom Rielly helped the producers of *[Prometheus](/wiki/Prometheus_(2012_film) "Prometheus (2012 film)")* gain approval for the use of the TED brand in the promotional short film *[TED 2023](/wiki/TED_2023 "TED 2023")*, designed by Rielly with [Ridley Scott](/wiki/Ridley_Scott "Ridley Scott") and [Damon Lindelof](/wiki/Damon_Lindelof "Damon Lindelof"), directed by [Luke Scott](/wiki/Luke_Scott_(director) "Luke Scott (director)"), and starring [Guy Pearce](/wiki/Guy_Pearce "Guy Pearce") as [Peter Weyland](/wiki/Peter_Weyland "Peter Weyland"), who in the film speaks at a fictional TED conference at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") in the then-future of 2023; on the film's release, Rielly noted that the association had generated millions of unique visits to the TED website.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In 2014, the conference was moved to [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"), British Columbia, Canada.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

TED is currently funded by various revenue streams, including attendance fees, corporate sponsorships, foundation support, licensing fees, and book sales. Sponsors do not participate in the event's creation and do not present on the main stage.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-americanu-29)

In 2015, TED staff consisted of about 180 people headquartered in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") and Vancouver, British Columbia.[[30]](#cite_note-30) On July 1, 2019, the TED Conferences LLC was transferred from Sapling Foundation to TED Foundation to "align with our brand and make it easier for our donors to connect TED donations to TED Conferences, LLC."[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

In 2021, TED launched the TED Audio Collective with a number of podcasts featuring previous TED Talks and other relevant topics.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

TED 2022 was held in Vancouver. There was criticism after [Marvin Rees](/wiki/Marvin_Rees "Marvin Rees"), [Mayor of Bristol](/wiki/Mayor_of_Bristol "Mayor of Bristol"), flew 9,200 miles (14,800 km) to speak about [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") and the need for reduced [carbon emissions](/wiki/Carbon_emission "Carbon emission").[[34]](#cite_note-BBC-61596817-34)

In 2025, it was announced that the annual conference would move to [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego"), California, beginning in 2027.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

## TED Prize

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=TED_(conference)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: TED Prize")]

The TED Prize was introduced in 2005. Until 2010, $100,000 was given annually to three individuals with a "wish to change the world".[[36]](#cite_note-36) Each winner unveiled their wish at the main annual conference. The winners from this period are as follows:

* 2005: [Bono](/wiki/Bono "Bono"), [Edward Burtynsky](/wiki/Edward_Burtynsky "Edward Burtynsky"),[[37]](#cite_note-37) [Robert Fischell](/wiki/Robert_Fischell "Robert Fischell")[[38]](#cite_note-TED2005-38)
* 2006: [Larry Brilliant](/wiki/Larry_Brilliant "Larry Brilliant"), [Jehane Noujaim](/wiki/Jehane_Noujaim "Jehane Noujaim"), [Cameron Sinclair](/wiki/Cameron_Sinclair "Cameron Sinclair")[[39]](#cite_note-TED2006-39)
* 2007: [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton"), [Edward O. Wilson](/wiki/Edward_O._Wilson "Edward O. Wilson"), [James Nachtwey](/wiki/James_Nachtwey "James Nachtwey")[[40]](#cite_note-TED2007-40)
* 2008: [Dave Eggers](/wiki/Dave_Eggers "Dave Eggers"), [Neil Turok](/wiki/Neil_Turok "Neil Turok"), [Karen Armstrong](/wiki/Karen_Armstrong "Karen Armstrong")[[41]](#cite_note-TED2008-41)
* 2009: [Sylvia Earle](/wiki/Sylvia_Earle "Sylvia Earle"), [Jill Tarter](/wiki/Jill_Tarter "Jill Tarter"), [José Antonio Abreu](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Antonio_Abreu "José Antonio Abreu")[[42]](#cite_note-TED2009-42)

Since 2010, a single winner has been chosen to ensure that TED can maximize its efforts in achieving the winner's wish. In 2012, the prize was not awarded to a person, but to a concept connected to the current global phenomenon of increasing [urbanization](/wiki/Urbanization "Urbanization"). In 2013, the prize amount was increased to $1 million.[[43]](#cite_note-43) The winners from this period are as follows:

* 2010: [Jamie Oliver](/wiki/Jamie_Oliver "Jamie Oliver")[[44]](#cite_note-TED2010-44)
* 2011: [JR](/wiki/JR_(artist) "JR (artist)")[[45]](#cite_note-TED2011-45)
* 2012: City 2.0[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-TED2012-47)
* 2013: [Sugata Mitra](/wiki/Sugata_Mitra "Sugata Mitra")[[48]](#cite_note-TED2013-48)
* 2014: [Charmian Gooch](/wiki/Charmian_Gooch "Charmian Gooch")[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-TED2014-50)
* 2015: [David Isay](/wiki/David_Isay "David Isay")[[51]](#cite_note-TED2015-51)
* 2016: Sarah Parcak[[52]](#cite_note-TED2016-52)
* 2017: [Raj Panjabi](/wiki/Raj_Panjabi "Raj Panjabi")[[53]](#cite_note-TED2017-53)

TED Conference commissioned New York artist [Tom Shannon](/wiki/Tom_Shannon_(artist) "Tom Shannon (artist)") to create a prize sculpture for all TED Prize winners. It consists of an eight-inch-diameter (20 cm) aluminum sphere magnetically levitated above a walnut disc.[[54]](#cite_note-54) As of 2018, the prize has been recast as [The Audacious Project](/wiki/The_Audacious_Project "The Audacious Project").[[55]](#cite_note-55)

## TED.com

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=TED_(conference)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: TED.com")]

In 2005, Chris Anderson hired [June Cohen](/wiki/June_Cohen "June Cohen") as Director of TED Media. In June 2006, after Cohen's idea of a TV show based on TED lectures was rejected by several networks, a selection of talks that had received highest audience ratings was posted on the websites of TED, [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") and [iTunes](/wiki/ITunes "ITunes") under [Creative Commons](/wiki/Creative_Commons_license "Creative Commons license") Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivs 3.0.[[56]](#cite_note-giving-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57) Only a handful of talks was initially posted to see if there was an audience for them. In January of the following year, the number of talks on the sites had grown to 44, and they had been viewed more than three million times. On the basis of that success, the organization pumped hundreds of thousands of dollars into its video production operations and the development of a website to feature about 100 of the talks.[[56]](#cite_note-giving-56)[[58]](#cite_note-CFP-58)

In April 2007, the new TED.com was launched, developed by New York and San Francisco-based design company [Method](/wiki/Method_(company) "Method (company)").[[59]](#cite_note-59) The website has won many prizes, including seven [Webby Awards](/wiki/Webby_Award "Webby Award"), iTunes' "Best Podcast of the Year" (2006–2010); the *[Communication Arts](/wiki/Communication_Arts_(magazine) "Communication Arts (magazine)")* Interactive Award for Information Design (2007); the OMMA Award for Video Sharing, the Web Visionary Award for Technical Achievement, and [The One Show Interactive](/wiki/The_One_Club "The One Club") Bronze Award (2008); the [AIGA](/wiki/AIGA "AIGA") Annual Design Competition (2009); and a [Peabody Award](/wiki/Peabody_Award "Peabody Award") (2012).[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63)

In January 2009, TED videos had been viewed 50 million times. In June 2011, they reached 500 million views.[[17]](#cite_note-mashable.com-17)

In March 2012, [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") announced a deal to [stream](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") an initial series of 16 two-hour collections of TED Talks on similar subjects. It was made available to subscribers in the United States, Canada, Latin America, the United Kingdom, and Ireland.[[64]](#cite_note-64) Hosted by Jami Floyd, *TED Talks NYC* debuted on [NYC Life](/wiki/NYC_Media "NYC Media") on March 21, 2012.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

### TEDGlobal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=TED_(conference)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: TEDGlobal")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/TEDGlobal2012.jpg/250px-TEDGlobal2012.jpg)](/wiki/File:TEDGlobal2012.jpg)

TEDGlobal 2012 at the [Edinburgh International Conference Centre](/wiki/Edinburgh_International_Conference_Centre "Edinburgh International Conference Centre")

In 2005, under Anderson's supervision, a more internationally oriented sister conference was added, under the name TEDGlobal. It was held, in chronological order: in [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford"), UK (2005), in [Arusha](/wiki/Arusha "Arusha"), Tanzania (2007, titled TEDAfrica), in Oxford again (2009 and 2010), and in [Edinburgh](/wiki/Edinburgh "Edinburgh"), UK (2011, 2012, and 2013). In 2014, it was held in [Rio de Janeiro, Brazil](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro,_Brazil "Rio de Janeiro, Brazil").[[69]](#cite_note-69) Additionally, there was TED India, in [Mysore](/wiki/Mysore "Mysore") (2009) and TEDGlobal London in [London](/wiki/London "London") (2015).[[70]](#cite_note-70) TEDGlobal 2017 was held again in Arusha, Tanzania, and it was curated and hosted by Emeka Okafor.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

TED's European director (and curator of TEDGlobal) is Swiss-born [Bruno Giussani](/wiki/Bruno_Giussani "Bruno Giussani").[[72]](#cite_note-72)

The TED 2011 conference, *The Rediscovery of Wonder*, was held in [Long Beach, California](/wiki/Long_Beach,_California "Long Beach, California"), US, from February 28 to March 4, 2011.[[73]](#cite_note-redorbit.com-73)[[74]](#cite_note-74) The TED conference has a companion conference, TEDGlobal, held in the UK each summer. The 2009 TEDGlobal, *The Substance of Things Not Seen*, was held in Oxford, July 21–24, 2009. 2010's TEDGlobal (again in Oxford) was themed *And Now The Good News*; in 2011 the conference moved to a new home in Edinburgh and was held July 12–15 with the theme *The Stuff Of Life*. The 2012 TEDGlobal conference *Radical Openness* was held in Edinburgh, June 25–29.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

### TEDx

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=TED_(conference)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: TEDx")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Quazi_Nawshaba_Ahmed_at_TEDxBAU.jpg/250px-Quazi_Nawshaba_Ahmed_at_TEDxBAU.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quazi_Nawshaba_Ahmed_at_TEDxBAU.jpg)

[Quazi Nawshaba Ahmed](/wiki/Quazi_Nawshaba_Ahmed "Quazi Nawshaba Ahmed") speaking at TEDx [Bangladesh Agricultural University](/wiki/Bangladesh_Agricultural_University "Bangladesh Agricultural University")

TEDx was founded by Lara Stein. TEDx are independent events similar to TED in presentation. They can be organized by anyone who obtains a free license from TED, and agrees to follow certain principles.[[16]](#cite_note-:0-16) Speakers are not paid and must relinquish the copyrights to their materials, which TED may edit and distribute under a non-commercial [Creative Commons](/wiki/Creative_Commons "Creative Commons") license (CC-BY-NC-SA).[[76]](#cite_note-76)[[77]](#cite_note-77)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/TEDxUTM_Conference_20250525_183.jpg/250px-TEDxUTM_Conference_20250525_183.jpg)](/wiki/File:TEDxUTM_Conference_20250525_183.jpg)

TEDx events are often accompanied by side activities such as workshops and performances, such as this one at TEDx[UTM](/wiki/University_of_Technology_Malaysia "University of Technology Malaysia").

By 2012, TEDx events ran at a global rate of about 5 per day, in 133 countries.[[78]](#cite_note-listen-78)

[TEDxPortland](/wiki/TEDxPortland "TEDxPortland") in Portland was the largest indoor TEDx event in the world, with more than 8,000 attendees, making it the largest TEDx conference in North America.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

In 2024, Raymond Rodriguez-Torres presented the TEDx talk *Tragedy to Triumph: Live Like Bella* about his daughter [Bella Rodriguez-Torres](/wiki/Bella_Rodriguez-Torres "Bella Rodriguez-Torres"), which received widespread attention online.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

A TEDx event hosted by Mumbai’s [Jai Hind College](/wiki/Jai_Hind_College "Jai Hind College") on February 7, 2024, featured a diverse lineup of industry leaders and experts discussing innovation and social impact, like [Vijay Kedia](/wiki/Vijay_Kedia "Vijay Kedia"), [Ramon Chibb](/wiki/Ramon_Chibb "Ramon Chibb") and [Riri Trivedi](/wiki/Riri_Trivedi "Riri Trivedi").[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82)

### TEDx Youth

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=TED_(conference)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: TEDx Youth")]

**TEDxYouth** is a programme of independently organised events licensed by TED that focus on engaging young people in sharing ideas and experiences. These events are typically organised by schools, youth groups, or community organisations, and feature speakers—often students or young professionals—alongside educators, artists, and innovators. Like other TEDx events, TEDxYouth conferences operate under free licences granted by TED, but are planned and coordinated locally. The format usually includes short talks, performances, and interactive activities designed to inspire creativity, critical thinking, and community involvement among younger audiences.

### TED Fellows

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=TED_(conference)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: TED Fellows")]

TED Fellows were introduced in 2007, during the first TEDAfrica conference in [Arusha](/wiki/Arusha "Arusha"), Tanzania, where 100 young people were selected from across the continent. Two years later, during TEDIndia, ninety-nine fellows were recruited, mainly from South Asia.

In 2009, the fellows program was initiated in its present form. For every TED or TEDGlobal conference, 20 fellows are selected; a total of 40 new fellows a year. Each year, 20 past fellows are chosen to participate in the two-year senior fellows program (in which they will attend four more conferences).

2019 marked the tenth anniversary of the TED Fellows program.

Acceptance as a fellow is not based on academic [credentials](/wiki/Credential "Credential"), but mainly on past and current actions, and plans for the future.[[83]](#cite_note-83) Besides attending a conference free of charge, each fellow takes part in a special program with [mentoring](/wiki/Mentorship "Mentorship") by experts in the field of spreading ideas, and can give a short talk on the "TED Fellows" stage. Some of these talks are subsequently published on TED.com. Senior fellows have additional benefits and responsibilities.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

### TED Audio Collective

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=TED_(conference)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: TED Audio Collective")]

The TED Audio Collective is a collection of podcasts with more than 25 shows.

One of those shows is the TED Interview [podcast](/wiki/Podcast "Podcast") that launched on October 16, 2018, during which [Chris Anderson](/wiki/Chris_Anderson_(entrepreneur) "Chris Anderson (entrepreneur)") holds conversations[[85]](#cite_note-85) with speakers who have previously given a [TED talk](/wiki/List_of_TED_speakers "List of TED speakers"),[[86]](#cite_note-86) providing the guest a chance to speak in greater depth about their background, projects, motivation,[[87]](#cite_note-87) re-evaluation of past experiences,[[88]](#cite_note-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89) or plans for the future.[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

### TEDMED

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=TED_(conference)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: TEDMED")]

Main article: [TEDMED](/wiki/TEDMED "TEDMED")

[TEDMED](/wiki/TEDMED "TEDMED") is an annual conference concerned with health and medicine. It is an independent event operating under license from the nonprofit TED conference.

In 2011, [Jay Walker](/wiki/Jay_S._Walker "Jay S. Walker") and a group of executives and investors purchased TEDMED from Hodosh for $16 million with future additional payments of as much as $9 million. The conference was then moved to Washington, DC.[[147]](#cite_note-147)

### Other programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=TED_(conference)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Other programs")]

* ***[TED Radio Hour](/wiki/TED_Radio_Hour "TED Radio Hour")***: A radio program,[[148]](#cite_note-148) with audio downloads and a podcast [RSS](/wiki/RSS "RSS") feed,[[149]](#cite_note-149) hosted by [Manoush Zomorodi](/wiki/Manoush_Zomorodi "Manoush Zomorodi"),[[150]](#cite_note-npr.org--about-npr-776132106-150) previously [Guy Raz](/wiki/Guy_Raz "Guy Raz"), and co-produced with [NPR](/wiki/NPR "NPR"). Each episode uses multiple TED Talks to examine a common theme.[[151]](#cite_note-151) Originated and executive produced by Deron Triff and [June Cohen](/wiki/June_Cohen "June Cohen"), the first episode was broadcast in 2012.[[152]](#cite_note-152)[[153]](#cite_note-153)

## Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=TED_(conference)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Criticism")]

### Pricing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=TED_(conference)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Pricing")]

[Sarah Lacy](/wiki/Sarah_Lacy "Sarah Lacy") of *[BusinessWeek](/wiki/BusinessWeek "BusinessWeek")* and *[TechCrunch](/wiki/TechCrunch "TechCrunch")* wrote in 2010 that TED attendees complained of elitism from a "hierarchy of parties throughout the LA area with strict lists and security" after the sessions. She gave credit for freely live-streaming and posting videos of its talks.[[155]](#cite_note-techcrunch.com-155)

### TED Talk content

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=TED_(conference)&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: TED Talk content")]

Disagreements have occurred between TED speakers and organizers. In her 2010 TED Talk, comedian [Sarah Silverman](/wiki/Sarah_Silverman "Sarah Silverman") referred to adopting a "[retarded](/wiki/Retard_(pejorative) "Retard (pejorative)")" child. TED organizer [Chris Anderson](/wiki/Chris_Anderson_(entrepreneur) "Chris Anderson (entrepreneur)") objected via his [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter") account, leading to a conflict between them conducted over Twitter.[[156]](#cite_note-156)[[157]](#cite_note-157)

Also in 2010, statistician [Nassim Taleb](/wiki/Nassim_Taleb "Nassim Taleb") called TED a "monstrosity that turns scientists and thinkers into low-level entertainers, like circus performers". He claimed TED curators did not initially post his talk "warning about the financial crisis" on their site on purely cosmetic grounds.[[158]](#cite_note-158)

In May 2012, venture capitalist [Nick Hanauer](/wiki/Nick_Hanauer "Nick Hanauer") spoke at [TED University](/wiki/TED_University "TED University"), challenging the belief that top income earners in America were the engines of job creation. TED attracted controversy when it chose not to post Hanauer's talk on their website. His talk analysed the top rate of tax versus unemployment and economic equality.[[159]](#cite_note-159) TED was accused of censoring the talk by not posting it.[[160]](#cite_note-IBT-160)[[161]](#cite_note-161)

On May 7, 2012, TED curator Chris Anderson, in an email to Hanauer, commented on his decision and took issue with several of Hanauer's assertions in the talk, including the idea that businesspeople were not job creators. He also made clear his aversion to the talk's "political nature".[[162]](#cite_note-email-162)

The *[National Journal](/wiki/National_Journal "National Journal")* reported that Anderson considered Hanauer's talk one of the most politically controversial they had produced, and they needed to be careful about when they posted it.[[160]](#cite_note-IBT-160) Anderson responded on his personal blog that TED posted only one talk each day, selected from many.[[163]](#cite_note-163) *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* staff writer Bruce Upbin noted that Hanauer's claim of a relationship between tax rates and unemployment was based entirely on falsified unemployment data,[[164]](#cite_note-164) while *[New York](/wiki/New_York_(magazine) "New York (magazine)")* magazine condemned TED's move.[[165]](#cite_note-165)

TEDx talks have been criticised for having a lower quality control than actual TED talks, with a number of TEDx talks being ridiculed by critics for promoting [pseudoscience](/wiki/Pseudoscience "Pseudoscience"). *Wired* and the *Harvard Business Review* suggested that this lack of quality control in TEDx talks damaged the broader TED brand.[[166]](#cite_note-166)[[167]](#cite_note-167)

Following a TEDx talk by parapsychologist [Rupert Sheldrake](/wiki/Rupert_Sheldrake "Rupert Sheldrake"), TED issued a statement saying their scientific advisors believed that "there is little evidence for some of Sheldrake's more radical claims", and recommended that it "should not be distributed without being framed with caution". The video was moved from the TEDx YouTube channel to the TED blog, accompanied by such framing language. This prompted accusations of censorship, which TED rebutted by pointing out that Sheldrake's talk was still on their website.[[168]](#cite_note-168)[[169]](#cite_note-169) A 2013 talk by [Graham Hancock](/wiki/Graham_Hancock "Graham Hancock"), promoting the use of the drug [DMT](/wiki/N,N-Dimethyltryptamine "N,N-Dimethyltryptamine"), was treated the same way.[[170]](#cite_note-170)[[171]](#cite_note-a_fresh_take-171)

According to Professor [Benjamin Bratton](/wiki/Benjamin_H._Bratton "Benjamin H. Bratton") at [University of California, San Diego](/wiki/University_of_California,_San_Diego "University of California, San Diego"), TED Talks' efforts at fostering progress in [socio-economics](/wiki/Socio-economics "Socio-economics"), science, philosophy and technology have been ineffective.[[172]](#cite_note-172) Chris Anderson responded that some critics misunderstood TED's goals, failing to recognise that it aimed to instill excitement in audiences in the same ways speakers felt it. He said that TED wished only to bring awareness of significant topics to larger audiences.[[173]](#cite_note-173)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=TED_(conference)&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

The [*Alien* franchise](/wiki/Alien_(franchise) "Alien (franchise)") features a fictional portrayal of a 2023 TED Conference, in the form of a [short film](/wiki/Alien_(franchise)#Short_films "Alien (franchise)") called "The Peter Weyland Files: TED Conference, 2023".[[174]](#cite_note-174) It was a part of the [viral marketing campaign](/wiki/TED_2023 "TED 2023") for the franchise's film [*Prometheus*](/wiki/Prometheus_(2012_film) "Prometheus (2012 film)") (2012).

[Episode 08](/wiki/Family_Guy_season_20#ep377 "Family Guy season 20"), season 20 of the animated TV series *[Family Guy](/wiki/Family_Guy "Family Guy")* features a cutaway scene of [Peter Griffin](/wiki/Peter_Griffin "Peter Griffin") giving a TED talk about birthdays.

Australian alternative rock band [TISM](/wiki/TISM "TISM") parodied TED talks at their 2 March 2024 concert in [Launceston, Tasmania](/wiki/Launceston,_Tasmania "Launceston, Tasmania"), as "TISM Talks", which included a skit parodying TED talks running behind the band for the duration of the show.

Episode 16 of series 2 of the TV series *[Elementary](/wiki/Elementary_(TV_series) "Elementary (TV series)")*, "The One Percent Solution", features ex-[Scotland Yard](/wiki/Scotland_Yard "Scotland Yard") [Inspector Gareth Lestrade](/wiki/Inspector_Lestrade "Inspector Lestrade") presenting a "DUG Chat". According to a tweet from the @ELEMENTARYstaff Twitter account,[[175]](#cite_note-175) "We had to call them 'Dug' chats because we weren't allowed to use the name 'Ted Talk'".
